metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_5053
metadata.comment:
Rationale: Authors hypothesize that "The much smaller molecular size and fewer transmembrane segments of TMEM230 in comparison to known neurotransmitter transport proteins indicate that TMEM230 is likely to be a trafficking protein rather than a neurotransmitter transport protein in synaptic vesicles."

Figure 4: Cultured mouse midbrain primary neurons were cotransfected with expression vectors encoding VAMP2 fused to eGFP (pEGFP-VAMP2) and a tag-free version of wild-type TMEM230 or each of the TMEM230 mutants. "Live imaging showed that expression of all four Parkinson's disease-linked mutants led to significantly slower movement of synaptic vesicles, as shown by reduced transport speed, track length and displacement length, in comparison to cells expressing wild-type protein.". Experimental description: Results, Figure 4 legend: "Fluorescence images of synaptic vesicles in transfected primary neurons. Cultured mouse midbrain primary neurons were cotransfected with expression vectors encoding VAMP2 fused to eGFP (pEGFP-VAMP2) and a tag-free version of wild-type TMEM230 or each of the TMEM230 mutants (in the pIRES2-ZsGreen1 vector)"

The mutant constructs used to demonstrate the phenotype were actually expressing human protein sequences;
"Two cDNA clones containing the full-length human TMEM230 coding sequences (RG219820, isoform 1; RG213024, isoform 2) were used as templates for construction of these expression constructs."

Doesn't matter in practise; this protein is very strongly conserved. Mouse and human protein sequences are both 120 amino-acids long and protein alignment using uniprot webtool showed only 4 AA differ.
